planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; WARNING: unripe green berries and all green parts contain solanine — a toxic alkaloid. Eat ONLY fully ripe, cooked berries and well-cooked young leaves. In Africa and Asia it is an important food plant when properly prepared; in Western gardens it is considered a weed.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; black nightshade is a widespread, weedy annual cosmopolitan plant whose cultural significance varies dramatically by geography — considered an undesirable toxic weed in Europe and North America, but an important traditional vegetable food plant across Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia, where the young leaves and fully ripe berries are eaten after cooking (boiling, sautéing, or stewing). The leaves are cooked as a pot herb (like spinach) across many African countries where it is called 'mnavu' (Swahili) or 'imboga' (various East African languages). The fully ripe, jet-black berries (glossy, round, about 1/4 inch) are cooked into sauces and stews or made into jam. IMPORTANT: unripe green berries contain toxic solanine and must never be eaten raw or unripe — only fully ripe (jet-black), well-cooked berries are safe. Young leaves are also cooked, not eaten raw. Self-sows readily in disturbed, rich soil. Tolerates partial shade.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; clammy ground cherry spreads by underground rhizomes and can become weedy in garden beds. Best in open areas where some naturalization is acceptable. The plant and all parts except the ripe berry inside the husk are toxic.

directions: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; clammy ground cherry is one of the most common and widespread native ground cherries of the eastern and central US — a perennial, rhizomatous plant producing the typical Physalis papery husk (the inflated calyx) surrounding a small, round, yellow berry when ripe. Named 'clammy' for the sticky, viscid glandular hairs covering the stems and leaves. The ripe yellow berries inside the papery husks have a pleasant, sweet-tart, slightly tropical flavor and are eaten fresh, used in jams, preserves, and pies, or dried like raisins. The fruits are ripe when the papery husk has turned tan and papery and the berry inside has turned from green to yellow. Like all Physalis, only the ripe berries are edible — unripe berries and all other plant parts contain toxic alkaloids. A weedy but edible native for natural gardens and food forests. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes. Tolerates partial shade — more shade-tolerant than tomatillo.

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; wonderberry grows quickly in warm weather and produces abundantly. Only fully ripe, black berries should be eaten — cooked or raw (some people cook them for better flavor). Do not confuse with true huckleberries (Vaccinium) — these are Solanums.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; wonderberry (also sold as 'garden huckleberry') is a Solanum species grown for its small, jet-black berries — promoted by plant breeder Luther Burbank in the early 1900s as 'the most delicious berry in the world' but received with disappointment because fresh berries have a bland, somewhat bitter, black-nightshade flavor. Cooked into pies, jams, and sauces (with generous sugar) the flavor improves considerably and is quite pleasant. An annual bush producing enormous quantities of marble-sized black berries through the growing season, making it productive even if the flavor is underwhelming fresh. Distinguished from black nightshade (S. nigrum) by its larger plant size, larger berries, and slightly different botanical characteristics. Like all black-berried Solanum, only fully ripe (jet-black) berries should be eaten; all green parts and unripe berries are potentially toxic. An easy-to-grow novelty edible for those interested in unusual fruits.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; hybrid flowering quince tolerates a wide range of soils and is very adaptable. Blooms most prolifically in full sun but tolerates considerable shade. Do not over-fertilize — excess nitrogen produces excessive leafy growth at the expense of flowering.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; hybrid flowering quince (Chaenomeles x superba) is the cross between the Japanese flowering quince (C. japonica, dwarf) and the Chinese flowering quince (C. speciosa, large) — a group of widely grown, compact, early-spring-flowering shrubs producing an outstanding display of flowers in red, pink, orange, salmon, white, or bicolor combinations before or as the leaves emerge. The parent of many of the most commonly sold cultivars including 'Crimson and Gold' (red with gold stamens), 'Jet Trail' (white), 'Nicoline' (scarlet), and 'Pink Lady' (salmon-rose). Blooms very early in the season — one of the first shrubs to flower in late winter/early spring — and the flowers are frost-tolerant. The small, hard, yellow-green fruits (like tiny hard quinces) that follow are edible when cooked and used for jellies, jams, and flavoring in some Asian and European cuisines. Thorny stems create good wildlife cover. One of the most dependable and widely grown early-spring flowering shrubs in temperate gardens.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; lemon guava is more cold-tolerant than tropical guava, surviving brief temperatures to 22-25°F when established. The yellow-fruited variety (var. lucidum) has a milder, sweeter flavor than the red-fruited strawberry guava. INVASIVE in Hawaii and parts of Florida.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun; lemon guava (yellow-fruited variety of strawberry guava) produces small, round to oval, yellow-skinned fruits with white flesh and a sweet, aromatic, guava-strawberry-lemon flavor that is milder and less acidic than the red-skinned strawberry guava (var. cattleyanum). The glossy, dark green, evergreen leaves and attractive smooth bark are ornamental year-round. Fruits eaten fresh, used in jams, jellies, and juices. Like the red strawberry guava, it is extremely adaptable and has become seriously invasive in Hawaii's native forests and in parts of Florida and the Caribbean, where it forms dense thickets that displace native vegetation. In cultivation outside of tropical areas (zone 8), it is well-behaved; in zone 9–11 it should be monitored for self-seeding. Birds are the primary seed dispersers. More cold-tolerant than tropical guava, growing in zone 8 with some frost protection. The lemon-yellow skin makes ripe fruits easy to identify for harvest.
